snippet: #[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]
/// Path to the JSONL file for a given namespace.
/// Meta-relationship types that operate on edges rather than spans.

payload.summary: The Fact enum is deliberately dumb — tagged JSON, two variants, no schema. This is the semantic web lesson applied: RDF/OWL died from over-specification. Damask survives by being as simple as <a href>. The format should resist added complexity even as capabilities grow.

Span is not code-specific. It pins a region of any text file — source code, legal contracts, research papers, policy documents. The path+lines+content_hash model works for any artifact. This generality is by design: damask is an annotation graph protocol, code is just the first use case.

The 10-signal hand-engineered ranking is the right v1 but should be understood as temporary. Per the bitter lesson, learned ranking (cross-encoder, trained on damask graphs) will eventually outperform hand-tuned weights. The graph structure is the durable asset; the ranking function is swappable. Do not over-engineer the ranking.

payload.action: Wire freshness into the query path and ranking signal #1 before any external demo
payload.summary: Freshness is the core differentiator vs every other agent memory solution — knowledge that knows when it is stale. But it is entirely unwired. Resolution, Recency, and Freshness types exist as pure framework with no downstream consumers. This is the feature that makes the pitch real; without it, damask is just structured notes.

payload.action: Design cross-repo span referencing without over-engineering
payload.summary: edges_file() hardcodes a single .damask/edges/ directory. But the long-term vision is cross-codebase graphs — a Node project with 1500 dependencies each carrying their own damask layers, stitched together. The current single-repo path resolution is the ceiling that needs lifting. How JSONL files from different repos compose is an open design question — solve it without adding semantic-web-level complexity.

The vocabulary and RelClass system is what makes damask capture comprehension rather than recall. Vector memory stores what was said; damask stores what was understood — typed as risks, decisions, invariants, dependencies. The classification into Judgment/Descriptive/Meta/Other is the mechanism by which observations become structured knowledge rather than conversation logs.

Edge.from and Edge.to are both Option — edges can float free without anchoring to any span. This is what enables philosophical or strategic observations that do not map cleanly to a code location. The annotation graph does not force you to point at something. This matters for the Glass Bead Game vision: some knowledge is about relationships between ideas, not about specific text regions.

Bootstrap cost is zero. A Claude Code instance given this CLI and a codebase autonomously produced 5 namespaces with 72 typed, scored observations — architecture, risks, decisions, invariants, spec-gaps — without being taught a schema or told what namespaces to create. The agent naturally emits structured comprehension when given the right primitive. This is the strongest evidence that damask is the right shape for how agents already think.

Damask graphs accumulated over thousands of codebases become a training dataset that does not exist today — a structured record of how code is built, how ideas become implementations, how risks are identified and resolved. This is the data flywheel: agents populate damask, damask data trains better agents, better agents populate better damask. The graph-as-learning-signal may ultimately be more valuable than the tool itself.

Market positioning: every agent memory solution (Mem0, Zep, LangGraph memory, vector stores) stores what was said. Damask stores what was understood. The differentiation is not in storage technology but in the shape of knowledge captured — typed, scored, decaying, location-anchored observations vs embedded conversation fragments. The moment you say agent memory people file you next to vector stores. Lead with comprehension externalized, not memory.

Context windows will grow to millions of tokens but will never hold the full dependency graph of a production system. node_modules alone drowns any context window. Damask solves this differently — not by loading everything, but by letting agents query a structured graph that threads through dependencies across codebases. This is a graph problem that context windows cannot solve by getting bigger.

Independently verified during genesis conversation — this invariant is what prevents damask from becoming a voting system. Both the spec (§7.6, §13.5) and a cold-start Claude instance flagged this as sacred.
Append-only confirmed as essential for multi-agent concurrency. Genesis conversation identified this as what makes damask work for Agent Teams — no locking, no conflicts, namespace-isolated appends.

Disposable index confirmed critical. Genesis conversation: rebuilding from JSONL means schema evolution is non-breaking. The full-rebuild performance issue is real but solvable without changing this invariant.
Meta-edges-as-edges confirmed by direct use — endorse/dispute/supersede operations during this conversation used the same graph primitives as content edges. The design is self-consistent.
Verified by reading build.rs and project.rs — JSONL is always the source, SQLite is always derived. No code path writes to SQLite without reading JSONL first.

Verified by reading vocabulary.rs, state.rs, and lint.rs — META_RELS is defined in vocabulary.rs:2 but the string literals are repeated independently in state.rs:39 and elsewhere. Three-location sync hazard is real.

Verified by reading state.rs — compute_active_state uses UPDATE queries that assume all edges already in DB. The 3-step process (all active → superseded inactive → meta inactive) would produce wrong results on partial data.

Verified by reading ranking.rs:84-93 — weights explicitly sum: 0.15+0.15+0.10+0.10+0.05+0.10+0.15+0.05+0.10+0.05 = 1.0. Per genesis conversation: these weights are temporary and should eventually become learned.
